content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

DATE: January 27, 1983

TO: Advice Request Meeting Participants

FROM: Lynn

Re: Request for Advice from Gordon Flowers, FTB, PRAD

Gordon Flowers has asked for advice regarding a situation that was disclosed in his audit of Clayton Jackson.

Clayton Jackson, a lobbyist and partner of General Consulting Company, borrowed $35,000 in 1981 from the firm to pay a personal tax liability. He expects to repay the loan in the first quarter of 1983 and the partners assured Gordon Flowers that they expect to be repaid.

Section 82045 defines "payments to influence legislative or administrative action" in part as:

(a) Direct or indirect payments to a lobbyist whether for salary, fee, compensation for expenses or any other purpose, by a person employing or contracting for the services of the lobbyist separately or jointly with other persons.

(b) Payment in support or assistance of a lobbyist or his activities, including but not limited to the direct payment of expenses incurred at the request or suggestion of the lobbyist.

Is the loan from General Consulting Company to Jackson for payment of his taxes for wages earned in part for lobbying a "payment to influence", and must either Jackson or the firm report the receipt or repayment of the loan?

Conclusion

Section 82044 defines a payment in part as ..."a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other

Rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value...". A payment (loan) is a "payment to influence legislative or administrative action" if it is for any of the purposes described in Section 82045. Section 82045 (a) defines "payments to influence legislative or administrative action" as:

    Direct or indirect payments to a lobbyist whether for salary, fee, compensation for expenses or any other purpose, by a person employing or contracting for the services of the lobbyist separately or jointly with other persons.

In the situation described above, the payment in question is a payment to a lobbyist (Clayton Jackson) by a person employing or contracting for the services of the lobbyist (General Consulting Company) and, therefore, is for the purpose described in Section 82045 (a).

Therefore, the loan should have been reported by General Consulting Company on its Lobbyist Employer Report, and by Mr. Jackson on his Lobbyist Report. General Consulting Company and Mr. Jackson may indicate the repayment by Mr. Jackson of the loan on their reports covering the period in which the repayment occurs.
